articles.title: AusAlert test date and time: emergency warning siren will blast over most phones in Australia on Monday. Here’s what to know
articles.description: National emergency test alert will be sent to all mobile devices on 27 July, with domestic violence advocates advising how to turn off safe phones Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast Virtually every phone in the country will blare an emergency siren at 2pm AEST on Monday 27 July as part of the first Australia-wide test of a new national warning system called AusAlert. related.description: Donald Trump has issued a new threat to destroy a bridge or power plant every time Iran attacks shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.

articles.title: How will Australia’s emergency services cope if satellites go down? With a sextant and a street directory
articles.description: Military and SES personnel being taught analog communication and navigation techniques as experts warn solar flare could knock out ‘critical space infrastructure’ Follow our Australia news live blog for latest updates Get our breaking news email, free app or daily news podcast Australia’s emergency services and military personnel are being trained to read paper maps, navigate without GPS support and sail by the stars in case something knocks out the nation’s space capabilities. articles.title: Money to burn? Big Tech’s AI splurge is blowing out
articles.description: The market reaction to the results of Google’s parent Alphabet and Elon Musk’s Tesla suggests that investors are becoming increasingly wary of the tech giants’ ballooning spending on AI.

articles.title: Breaking: Woodside fails in court to force activists to hand over stink-bomb plans
articles.description: Energy giant Woodside fails in its Supreme Court bid to force climate activists to hand over documents about the planning of a stink-bomb protest which prompted the evacuation of its Perth headquarters.

articles.title: Mamdani says he cannot arrest Netanyahu in New York but US government can

articles.title: Indigenous-owned town hopes giant fish statue will attract tourists
articles.description: On the far west coast of SA, this Aboriginal community requires a permit to enter, meaning people usually drive on by. But new public art at the roadhouse, bought by the community, aims to boost tourism and financial independence.

articles.title: Bridge operator opts to paint over rather than wash off Pam the Bird

articles.title: Record $60k fine for man behind 'brazen' dump site that will cost $1m to clean
articles.description: The owner of one of Victoria's worst illegal dump sites has been handed a record fine of $60,000 and ordered to pay an expected $1 million to clean up the polluted property in Melbourne's outer north-east.

articles.title: How do Lime’s ebikes make money? Share market debut sheds light on finances
articles.description: The firm’s public listing in June gives an insight into a question analysts have been wondering for years: how does this business stack up? In April, Lime brought its green ebikes and e-scooters to Canberra. Before it had even arrived, the US micro-mobility firm had invested nearly US$2m. Lime’s experience in Australia’s sprawling capital is emblematic of a broader problem at the core of a business heavy on capital outlay. Continue reading...

articles.title: Pauline Hanson’s comments about domestic violence are more than vile. They are dangerous for women | Nicole Yade
articles.description: A woman in Australia is killed by someone she trusted every 11 days and misinformation about family violence does real harm to victims On Wednesday, senator Pauline Hanson claimed domestic and family violence is “a two-way street”, blamed the cost of living for perpetrators’ actions, and suggested women experiencing abuse should simply “not put up with it”.
